title: Post-LN vs. Pre-LN Architectures
concept: Post-LN places normalization after the residual addition (original Transformer); Pre-LN normalizes activations before the layer, making deep models easier to train without warmups.

exercises.prompt: What is the main goal of Layer Normalization in a Transformer?

options.text: To keep signal values scaled and stable
To make the model run faster on CPUs
To delete useless words from the input

exercises.explain: Layer Normalization (LN) rescales activations to prevent them from becoming too large or too small during training.

cards.title: The Secret of Stable Training
cards.body: When researchers first tried to build deeper Transformers, they kept crashing during training. The secret to fixing it wasn't a bigger computer, but simply moving the 'Normalization' step to a different spot in the loop!

cards.title: Post-LN vs. Pre-LN
cards.body: In a Transformer layer, Layer Normalization (LN) keeps numbers at a healthy scale. Post-LN (the original way) normalizes the data *after* adding the residual connection. Pre-LN normalizes the data *before* it goes into the attention or feed-forward blocks, keeping gradients much more stable in deep networks.

cards.title: The Block Tower Story
cards.body: Imagine building a tower of blocks. Post-LN is like stacking the blocks first, and then trying to level the whole tower. If the tower is too tall, it might tip over before you can stabilize it! Pre-LN is like leveling and sanding each block *before* adding it to the stack. The tower stays straight and stable the entire time you are building it.

cards.title: How GPT-3 Stayed Stable
cards.body: In early models like the original BERT (Post-LN), training would instantly fail (diverge) if the learning rate was too high at the start, requiring a careful 'warmup'. Modern models like GPT-3 use Pre-LN, allowing them to train stably from step one without crashing.

cards.title: Is Newer Always Worse?
cards.body: Many believe that because Post-LN was in the original Transformer paper, it must always be the best choice. In reality, almost all modern, ultra-deep models use Pre-LN because it is far easier to train without the training crashing.

cards.title: Mapping the Flow
cards.body: Let's look at how data flows through a single layer in both styles. Notice where the Normalization (LN) block sits relative to the Sub-layer (Attention/MLP).

left.text: Inputs are normalized FIRST, then sent to the sub-layer and added to the shortcut.

right.text: Inputs go to the sub-layer, get added to the shortcut, and the sum is normalized LAST.

exercises.prompt: Where does the Post-LN architecture place the normalization step?

options.text: It places normalization before the attention block
It places normalization after the residual addition
It completely removes the residual connection
It only normalizes the final output of the entire model

exercises.explain: Post-LN places the normalization step after the residual addition. Because of this, gradients can grow too large near the output, making deep models unstable without a learning rate warmup.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Pre-LN makes it easier to train very deep neural networks than Post-LN.
exercises.explain: Pre-LN is widely used in modern LLMs because it allows stable training of networks with dozens or hundreds of layers without constant crashes.

exercises.prompt: Which architecture stabilizes deep networks by normalising inputs early?
exercises.sentence: Modern deep models use [blank] to keep training stable.
exercises.explain: Pre-LN applies normalization to the inputs before they enter the attention and feed-forward layers, preventing gradients from exploding.

exercises.prompt:
An AI system diagrams two layers:
1. Input -> LN -> Sub-layer -> Add -> Output
2. Input -> Sub-layer -> Add -> LN -> Output

Which one is correct?

options.text: Diagram 1 is Post-LN, Diagram 2 is Pre-LN
Diagram 1 is Pre-LN, Diagram 2 is Post-LN

exercises.explain: The first diagram shows normalization (LN) happening directly on the input branch before the weight layers, which is Pre-LN. The second diagram shows normalization happening after the skip connection adds the output, which is Post-LN.

exercises.prompt: Why does Pre-LN make it much easier to train very deep Transformers?

options.text: It forces the model to use Post-LN anyway
It makes the model train slower by adding extra steps
It stabilizes the gradient flow, removing the need for a strict warmup phase
It reduces the total number of layers we can train

exercises.explain: Pre-LN normalizes the inputs right before the sub-layers (attention/feed-forward). This keeps gradients stable from the very first step, allowing us to train deep networks without needing a fragile warmup phase.
